aids.title: BFS is Like a Ripple in Water
aids.content: Drop a stone in a pond. The ripple expands outward in concentric circles -- first the closest points, then farther ones. BFS works the same way: it visits all vertices at distance 1, then distance 2, then distance 3, and so on.
aids.forRemembering: The level-by-level exploration behavior of BFS

aids.title: BFS = Broad First, Queue
aids.content: 'BFS = Broad First, Queue' -- BFS goes broad (all neighbors first) and uses a Queue. 'DFS = Daring First, Stack' -- DFS goes deep and uses a Stack. B comes before D alphabetically, Q comes before S alphabetically.
aids.forRemembering: BFS uses a queue while DFS uses a stack

aids.title: Mark Visited on Enqueue, Not Dequeue
aids.content: A common exam mistake: marking vertices visited when dequeued instead of when enqueued. If you mark on dequeue, the same vertex can be enqueued multiple times, wasting time and space. Always mark visited WHEN you first discover and enqueue.
aids.forRemembering: When to mark a vertex as visited during BFS

aids.title: BFS Shortest Path Only for Unweighted
aids.content: BFS gives shortest paths only in unweighted graphs (or graphs where all edges have the same weight). For weighted graphs, you need Dijkstra's algorithm. This is a common exam trick question.
aids.forRemembering: The limitation of BFS for shortest path computation
